blocks:
Ayahs

تَبَّتْ يَدَآ أَبِى لَهَبٍ وَتَبَّ (١)
Perish the hands of Abu Lahab, and perish he! (1)
مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُ مَالُهُۥ وَمَا كَسَبَ (٢)
Neither his wealth availed him, nor what he had earned. (2)
سَيَصْلَىٰ نَارًا ذَاتَ لَهَبٍ (٣)
Soon he will enter the blazing fire, (3)
وَٱمْرَأَتُهُۥ حَمَّالَةَ ٱلْحَطَبِ (٤)
and his wife [too], the firewood carrier, (4)
فِى جِيدِهَا حَبْلٌ مِّن مَّسَدٍۭ (٥)
with a rope of palm fibre around her neck. (5)

Content and Virtue of Surah "Tabbat"

This Surah, which was revealed in Makkah and approximately during the early stages of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) open invitation, is the only Surah in which a severe attack is launched against one of the enemies of Islam and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) of that era by name (namely Abu Lahab). Its content demonstrates that he harbored a particular enmity toward the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), and neither he nor his wife spared any form of sabotage or foul language.

The Quran explicitly states that both are inhabitants of Hell and there is no way of salvation for them. This reality came to pass; ultimately, both died without faith, and this stands as an explicit prophecy of the Quran.

In a Hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), it is mentioned:

من قرأها رجوت ان لا يجمع اللَّه بينه و بين ابى لهب فى دار واحدة:

"Whoever recites it, I hope that Allah will not gather him and Abu Lahab in the same abode." (Meaning he will be among the people of Paradise while Abu Lahab is in Hell) [^1]. It is self-evident that this virtue belongs to one who, by reciting this Surah, separates his path from that of Abu Lahab, not those who recite it with their tongues but act like Abu Lahab.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 558.

Occasion of Revelation

It is narrated from Ibn Abbas: When the verse "And warn your closest kindred" (26:214) was revealed and the Prophet was commissioned to warn his near relatives and invite them to Islam (to make his call public), the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) went atop Mount Safa and cried out: "Ya Sabahah!" (This was a phrase Arabs used when facing a surprise enemy attack; to alert everyone to confront it, someone would shout "Ya Sabahah!". The word "Sabah" [morning] was chosen because surprise attacks often occurred at dawn).

When the people of Makkah heard this cry, they asked: "Who is shouting?"

It was said, "It is Muhammad." The crowd gathered toward him. He called the Arab tribes by name, and they assembled at his voice. He said: "Tell me, if I were to inform you that enemy riders are attacking you from the side of this mountain, would you believe me?"

They replied: "We have never heard a lie from you."

He said: انى نذيرلَكُمْ بَيْنَ يَدَيْ عَذابٍ شَدِيدٍ: "I am a warner to you in the face of a severe divine punishment" (inviting you to monotheism and to abandon idols). When Abu Lahab heard this, he said: "Perish you! Did you gather us only for this?!" (تبا لك! أ ما جمعتنا الا لهذا؟!). It was at this moment that this Surah was revealed: "تَبَّتْ يَدا أَبِي لَهَبٍ وَ تَبَّ" (May the hands of Abu Lahab perish, and may he perish!).

Some have added here that when Abu Lahab's wife (named Umm Jamil) learned that this Surah was revealed about her and her husband, she came looking for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). While she could not see him, she had a stone in her hand and said: "I have heard that Muhammad has satirized me; by Allah, if I find him, I will strike his mouth with this stone! I am a poet myself!" Then she recited verses disparaging the Prophet and Islam [^1]. The danger of Abu Lahab and his wife to Islam and their enmity was not limited to this; the reason the Quran directs its sharpest attack toward them and explicitly censures them involves more reasons that will be mentioned later, Allah willing.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 7324 (slightly summarized). The same theme with variations is narrated by Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Ibn Athir in Al-Kamil Vol. 2 p. 60, Durr al-Manthur, Abu al-Futuh Razi, Fakhr Razi, and Fi Zilal al-Quran in the commentary of this Surah.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ تَبَّتْ يَدا أَبِي لَهَبٍ وَ تَبَّ (1) ما أَغْنى‌ عَنْهُ مالُهُ وَ ما كَسَبَ (2) سَيَصْلى‌ ناراً ذاتَ لَهَبٍ (3) وَ امْرَأَتُهُ حَمَّالَةَ الْحَطَبِ (4) فِي جِيدِها حَبْلٌ مِنْ مَسَدٍ (5)

Commentary

Perish the Hands of Abu Lahab!

As mentioned regarding the occasion of revelation, this Surah is in fact a response to the foul words of "Abu Lahab," the uncle of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and son of Abdul Muttalib, who was a fierce enemy of Islam. Upon hearing the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) open and public invitation and his warning of divine punishment, he said: "Perish and ruin be upon you, did you call us for this?!" In response to this foul-mouthed man, the Holy Quran says: "Perish both hands of Abu Lahab, and may he perish/suffer loss" (تَبَّتْ يَدا أَبِي لَهَبٍ وَ تَبَّ).

"Tabb" and "Tabab" (rhyming with kharab), according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, mean continuous and lasting loss. However, Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan says it means a loss that leads to destruction. Some linguists have also interpreted it as "to cut off," perhaps because continuous loss leading to destruction naturally causes a severance. From the sum of these meanings, the interpretation we provided for the verse is derived.

Of course, this destruction and loss can be worldly, spiritual/otherworldly, or both.

Here, the question arises: How did the Holy Quran, contrary to its usual method and practice, mention a person by name and attack him with such intensity?

The answer becomes clear once Abu Lahab's position is understood. His name was "Abd al-Uzza" (servant of the idol Uzza) and his kunya (agnomen) was "Abu Lahab." This kunya was perhaps chosen for him because he had a red, glowing face, as Lahab linguistically means a flame of fire.

He and his wife, "Umm Jamil," who was the sister of Abu Sufyan, were among the harshest and most foul-mouthed enemies of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله).

In a narration, a person named Tariq al-Muharibi says: "I was in the market of Dhu al-Majaz (near Arafat, a short distance from Makkah) when I suddenly saw a young man calling out: 'O people! Say: There is no god but Allah, so that you may succeed.' And I saw a man behind him striking his heels with stones such that blood flowed from his feet, shouting: 'O people! This man is a liar, do not believe him!' I asked, 'Who is this youth?' They said, 'It is Muhammad, who claims to be a prophet, and this old man is his uncle Abu Lahab, who considers him a liar.'" [^1]

In another report, Rabi'ah ibn Abbad says: "I was with my father and saw the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) going to the Arab tribes, calling each one and saying: 'I am the Messenger of Allah to you; worship none but the One God, and associate nothing with Him...' When he finished his speech, a cross-eyed, handsome man behind him would shout: 'O tribe of so-and-so! This man wants you to abandon the idols Lat and Uzza and your allies among the Jinn, and follow his innovation and misguidance; do not listen to his words and do not follow him!' I asked who he was, and they said: 'His uncle Abu Lahab.'" [^1]

In another report, we read: Whenever a group of Arabs from outside Makkah entered the city, they would go to Abu Lahab because of his kinship to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his advanced age, inquiring about the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله). He would say: "Muhammad is a sorcerer." They would then return without meeting the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). Once, a group came and said: "We will not leave Makkah until we see him." Abu Lahab said: "We are constantly busy treating his madness! Perish he!" [^2]

From these narrations, it is well understood that he followed the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) like a shadow on many occasions and spared no effort in sabotage. He particularly had a foul and polluted tongue, using crude and offensive expressions, and in this regard, he was perhaps considered the foremost of all enemies of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله). For this reason, the verses under discussion criticize him and his wife Umm Jamil with such explicitness and harshness.

He was the only one who did not sign the Banu Hashim's pact to protect the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), instead joining the ranks of his enemies and participating in the enemies' alliances. Given these facts, the reason for the exceptional nature of this Surah becomes clear.

Then it adds: "His wealth and what he has earned have not profited him at all, nor will they ward off divine punishment from him" (ما أَغْنى‌ عَنْهُ مالُهُ وَ ما كَسَبَ). [^1] From this expression, it is understood that he was a proud, wealthy man who relied on his assets and riches in his anti-Islamic efforts.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 559. [^1]: Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 8, p. 697. [^2]: Tafsir al-Furqan, Vol. 30, p. 503. [^1]: "Ma" in "ma kasab" can be mawsulah (relative) or masdariyah (infinitive). Some grant it a broad meaning including not just wealth but also his children. The "ma" in "ma aghna" is certainly nafiyah (negation).

In the next verse, it adds: "He will soon enter a Fire of blazing flames" (سَيَصْلى‌ ناراً ذاتَ لَهَبٍ). If his name was "Abu Lahab" (Father of Flame), the fire of his punishment is also "Abu Lahab" and has great flames (note that "Lahab" here is indefinite, indicating the greatness of that flame).

Not only Abu Lahab, but no disbeliever or evildoer will find their wealth, riches, or social status saving them from the fire of Hell and divine punishment, as we read in verses 88 and 89 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara: "The Day when neither wealth nor sons will avail, except him who brings to Allah a clean heart" (a soul with faith and piety).

Certainly, the fire intended in the verse "سَيَصْلى‌ ناراً ذاتَ لَهَبٍ" is the fire of Hell, but some have suggested it might also include the fire of this world.

It is mentioned in narrations that after the Battle of Badr and the severe defeat suffered by the polytheists of Quraish, Abu Lahab—who had not personally participated in the battlefield—asked Abu Sufyan about the events upon his return.

Abu Sufyan described how the Quraish army was defeated and crushed, then added: "By Allah, in this battle, we saw riders between heaven and earth who had come to Muhammad's aid!" At this, Abu Rafi', a servant of Abbas, says: "I was sitting there, I raised my hand and said: 'Those were the angels of heaven.'"

Abu Lahab became extremely enraged and struck my face hard, then lifted me and slammed me to the ground, beating me continuously out of his inner anguish. Abbas's wife, Umm al-Fadl, was present; she took a wooden pole and struck Abu Lahab hard on the head, saying: "Have you found this weak man alone?!" Abu Lahab's head was fractured and bled. After seven days, his body became infected, and sores like "plague" appeared on his skin, and he died of that same illness.

The stench of his body was such that people did not dare approach him. They took him outside Makkah, poured water on him from a distance, and then threw stones at him until his body was hidden under stones and earth! [^1]

In the next verse, it addresses the state of his wife "Umm Jamil," saying: "And his wife [too] shall enter the blazing fire of Hell, while she carries firewood" (وَ امْرَأَتُهُ حَمَّالَةَ الْحَطَبِ). [^2]

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 19, p. 227. [^2]: "Imra'atuhu" is conjoined to the hidden pronoun in "sayasla," and "hammalat al-hatab" is a hal (circumstantial clause) and is mansub (accusative). Some, like Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf, consider it mansub for the purpose of "censure" (dhamm). However, the first meaning is certainly better.

"And while around her neck is a rope or necklace of palm-fiber!" (فِي جِيدِها حَبْلٌ مِنْ مَسَدٍ).

There is no dispute that Abu Lahab's wife, who was the sister of Abu Sufyan and the paternal aunt of Mu'awiyah, participated in her husband's enmities and sabotage against Islam. However, regarding why the Quran describes her as Hammalat al-Hatab (the woman who carries firewood), several interpretations have been mentioned:

Some have said it is because she used to carry bundles of thorns on her back and scatter them in the path of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) to injure his blessed feet.

Others have said this expression is a metaphor for her tale-bearing and slander, just as in Persian literature this same expression is used for slander: "War between two people is like fire * The wretched slanderer is the wood-carrier!"

Some also consider it a metaphor for her extreme stinginess; despite all her wealth, she was unwilling to help the needy, and for this reason, she was likened to a poor "wood-carrier."

Others say that on the Day of Resurrection, she will carry the burden of the sins of a large group on her back.

Among these meanings, the first is the most appropriate, though a combination of them is not unlikely.

Jid (rhyming with did) means "neck," and its plural is ajyad. Some linguists believe that jid, unuq, and raqabah all have similar meanings, with the difference that jid refers to the upper part of the chest, unuq to the back of the neck or the whole neck, and raqabah to the neck itself, and sometimes it refers to a human being, as in "فَكُّ رَقَبَةٍ" meaning the freeing of a human. [^1] Masad (rhyming with hasad) means a rope woven from fibers. Some have said masad is a rope placed around her neck in Hell that has the roughness of fibers, the heat of fire, and the weight of iron!

Some have also said that since aristocratic women find their identity in jewelry, especially expensive necklaces, Allah will cast a necklace of palm-fiber around the neck of this arrogant aristocratic woman in the Hereafter to humiliate her, or it is a metaphor for her humiliation in general.

Others have said the reason for this expression is that Umm Jamil had an expensive jeweled necklace and had sworn to spend it on enmity toward the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله); therefore, as a punishment for this act, Allah decreed such a torment for her.

Key Points

1. Yet Another Sign of the Quran's Miraculous Nature

We know these verses were revealed in Makkah, and the Quran decisively announced that Abu Lahab and his wife would be in the fire of Hell—meaning they would never embrace faith. And so it was. Many of the polytheists of Makkah truly believed, and some did so outwardly, but among those who believed neither in reality nor outwardly were these two. This is one of the reports of the unseen (ghayb) in the Holy Quran. The Quran contains other such reports, which form a chapter on the Quran's miracles under the title "Unseen Reports of the Quran," and we have provided appropriate discussions under each of these verses.

2. Answer to a Question

A question arises here: with this prophecy of the Holy Quran, it was no longer possible for Abu Lahab and his wife to believe, otherwise this report would have become false.

This question is similar to the famous question regarding "Allah's Knowledge" in the discussion of predestination (jabr): that is, we know that Allah, who has known everything from eternity, also knew the sins of the sinners and the obedience of the obedient. Therefore, if a sinner does not sin, Allah's knowledge would become ignorance!

Muslim scholars and philosophers have answered this question since ancient times: Allah knows what each person will do using their own choice and freedom. For example, in the verses under discussion, Allah knew from the beginning that Abu Lahab and his wife, by their own will and volition, would never believe—not through compulsion or necessity.

In other words, the element of free will and choice was also part of Allah's knowledge; He knew what action the servants would perform with the attribute of choice and by their own will. Certainly, such knowledge and reporting of such a future is an emphasis on the issue of free will, not a proof of compulsion (reflect on this).

3. Those Close Without Vision are Always Distant!

This Surah once again emphasizes the truth that kinship has the least value if it is not accompanied by a bond of ideology. The men of God showed no flexibility toward the deviant, the tyrants, and the rebellious, even if they were their closest relatives.

Despite Abu Lahab being the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) uncle and considered among his closest kin, when he separated his ideological, belief-based, and practical path from him, he came under the severest barrage of rebuke and censure like other deviants and the misguided. Conversely, there were distant individuals who were not only not relatives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) but were not even of his race or language, yet through intellectual, belief-based, and practical bonds, they became so close that, according to the famous Hadith "Salman is from us, the Ahl al-Bayt," they became as if part of the Prophet's family. [^1]

It is true that the verses of this Surah speak only of Abu Lahab and his wife, but it is clear that they are censured this way because of their attributes. Therefore, any individual or group possessing those same attributes will have a fate similar to theirs.

O Allah! Purify our hearts from all stubbornness and obstinacy.

O Lord! We are all fearful of the end of our affairs; grant us security and peace, and make the end of our affairs good.

O God! We know that in that Great Court, neither wealth nor kinship will avail; only Your grace is effective. Include us in Your graces.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Tabbat.

[^1]: Al-Tahqiq fi Kalimat al-Quran al-Karim, Vol. 2, p. 158. [^1]: Further explanation on this matter is provided in Volume 9, page 118, under verse 46 of Surah Hud, in connection with the account of the son of Noah (عليه السلام).
